description: This figure presents a multi-panel line graph showing the effect of aluminum content (3–10 wt.%) on the mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels containing 0.85% C (dashed curves) and 0.95% C (solid curves), thermally processed by water quenching from 1150°C followed by aging at 550°C for 16 h. The top panel plots mechanical strength: solid and open triangles represent ultimate tensile strength (UTS) and 0.2% yield strength (0.2% YS), respectively. Both UTS and YS remain relatively flat below approximately 7% Al but rise sharply at higher Al contents, with the 0.95% C alloy consistently outperforming the 0.85% C alloy. The middle panel displays ductility, where solid circles denote elongation (δ) and open circles denote reduction of area (ψ); both metrics stay fairly stable across most of the Al range before declining slightly above approximately 8% Al. The bottom panel shows U-notch impact toughness (K_CU) with solid and open diamonds corresponding to 0.85% C and 0.95% C, respectively; toughness peaks near 6–7% Al (approximately 180 J/cm² for 0.85% C and 160 J/cm² for 0.95% C) and then decreases at higher Al contents. The figure supports the conclusion that considerable age hardening occurs when Al exceeds approximately 7%, attributed to homogeneous κ-phase precipitation, whereas lower Al contents lead to grain-boundary carbide networks detrimental to toughness, thereby defining a compositional window for balanced structural performance.
